stem:
A 38-year-old woman with treatment-resistant major depression is brought to the emergency department by family after 4 days of progressive withdrawal. Today she has been mute, has not eaten or drunk, and has remained in awkward postures for hours. On examination she stares ahead without blinking, will hold an arm raised when placed there for over a minute, and shows a positive grasp reflex.

Her father remembers her stopping olanzapine 3 weeks ago. Bloods show a creatine kinase of 1500, normal renal function, normal calcium, and a venous pH of 7.39. Temperature is normal and there is no rigidity.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, BP 124/76, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile with waxy flexibility on positioning; staring without blinking; no tremor or rigidity
data_table.Investigations: CK 1500, UEC normal, calcium normal, glucose 4.8, urine drug screen negative; CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg now and reassess catatonia signs within 10 minutes
options.rationale: The lorazepam challenge of 1 to 2 mg intravenously confirms the diagnosis and provides the first-line treatment in benzodiazepine-responsive catatonia; a clear response supports continued scheduled lorazepam with ECT planned for non-response. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 25 mg for behavioural containment of agitation
options.rationale: Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome, particularly in a recently neuroleptic-exposed patient; behavioural agents are inappropriate when the picture is hypokinetic. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Commence intravenous dantrolene as empirical treatment for suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS is excluded by the absence of fever, rigidity, and autonomic instability; CK elevation alone in immobile catatonia does not justify dantrolene and delays specific therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Transfer to a medical ward for nutritional support and observation without psychiatric intervention
options.rationale: Catatonia carries significant mortality from dehydration, venous thromboembolism, and pneumonia if untreated; transfer without first-line lorazepam delays definitive care. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

options.text: Restart olanzapine at her previous maintenance dose with intravenous fluid support as needed
options.rationale: Resuming the antipsychotic she stopped 3 weeks ago risks NMS in this state and does not treat catatonia; lorazepam is the first-line agent regardless of underlying mood or psychotic disorder. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterised by stupor, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, and stereotypies, occurring across mood, psychotic, neurological, and medical conditions. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale supports diagnosis. First-line treatment is intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg as a challenge dose, with rapid response supporting continued scheduled benzodiazepine therapy. ECT is the definitive treatment for malignant or non-responsive catatonia. Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk neuroleptic malignant syndrome; they are avoided until catatonia resolves.
key_points: First-line treatment is intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg as a challenge dose
Antipsychotics are avoided as they worsen catatonia and risk NMS
ECT is the definitive treatment when benzodiazepines fail
Catatonia is missed when CK elevation triggers a presumed NMS workup without checking catatonic signs
explanation.pearls: Bush-Francis score >=2 plus a positive lorazepam challenge defines treatable catatonia. Plan ECT early if no response within 24 to 48 hours.

stem: A 4-day-old term boy is referred by the midwife after his mother noticed two episodes of green vomit overnight. He was born at 39 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, passed meconium at 12 hours, and had been breastfeeding well. On arrival to the neonatal review clinic he is alert and pink but mildly distressed. His abdomen is soft and minimally distended without peritonism. Capillary refill is 2 seconds and his vital signs are normal for age.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 48, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 36.8, BP 70/40
data_table.Examination: Alert and pink; mild abdominal distension; soft non-tender abdomen; normally placed anus; passing meconium
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas: pH 7.36, lactate 1.6; FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph and observe with hourly abdominal examinations in the paediatric unit
options.rationale: A plain abdominal film cannot reliably exclude malrotation with midgut volvulus; relying on it risks missing an evolving volvulus that progresses to bowel necrosis within hours. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study to exclude malrotation with midgut volvulus
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise; an upper GI contrast study is the diagnostic investigation of choice and must be expedited as delay risks irreversible bowel ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Trial of a thickened amino acid based formula and dietitian review for cow's milk protein allergy
options.rationale: Cow's milk protein allergy does not present with bilious vomiting in the first week; trialling formula change delays the urgent diagnosis of a surgical cause. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Commence ondansetron and IV maintenance fluids and reassess in 12 hours in the short stay unit
options.rationale: Symptomatic antiemetic therapy without investigation is dangerous in any neonate with bilious vomiting; the diagnostic priority is to exclude volvulus by contrast study. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound to assess the superior mesenteric artery and vein relationship and bowel perfusion
options.rationale: Doppler ultrasound of the SMA and SMV relationship may suggest malrotation but is operator dependent and is not the primary investigation; an upper GI contrast study remains the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

explanation.summary: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with midgut volvulus until imaging proves otherwise. Even in a well-appearing infant the diagnosis is time critical because volvulus can cause irreversible bowel necrosis within hours. The diagnostic investigation of choice is an upper gastrointestinal contrast study, which demonstrates the duodenojejunal flexure position and any corkscrew appearance of the duodenum. Concurrent management is nil by mouth, nasogastric drainage, intravenous fluids, and immediate referral to paediatric surgery. Plain films and abdominal ultrasound are adjuncts that do not exclude the diagnosis.
key_points: Bilious vomiting in the newborn equals malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise
Upper GI contrast study is the primary investigation
Even a well infant needs urgent imaging because volvulus progresses quickly
Initial care includes NBM, NG drainage, IV fluids and paediatric surgical referral
explanation.pearls: Bile is green. A normal abdominal exam in a green-vomiting neonate does not exclude volvulus. Move directly to the contrast study, not the AXR.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Bilious Vomiting in the Neonate

stem: A 31-year-old woman returns to her general practice 9 weeks after the birth of her second baby with anxiety, palpitations, sweating, and 3 kg weight loss despite a normal appetite. She breastfeeds 8 times per day and her baby is thriving. She has no goitre or eye signs. She is taking no medications. Her thyroid function tests show TSH less than 0.01 mIU/L, free T4 30 pmol/L, free T3 8 pmol/L. Anti-TPO antibodies are strongly positive. A radioactive iodine uptake scan performed off breastfeeding milk donation shows reduced uptake throughout the gland.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104, BP 132/78, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.9
data_table.Examination: Fine resting tremor; warm peripheries; no goitre; no ophthalmopathy; no pretibial myxoedema
data_table.Investigations: TSH < 0.01 mIU/L, fT4 30 pmol/L, fT3 8 pmol/L, anti-TPO positive, low-uptake scan

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence carbimazole 15 mg daily and titrate to maintain free T4 in the upper normal range
options.rationale: Postpartum thyroiditis is not driven by new hormone synthesis; thionamides have no role because the thyrotoxicosis is from preformed hormone release and remits spontaneously. (Source: ESA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guidance)

options.text: Refer urgently for radioactive iodine ablation while ceasing breastfeeding for 6 weeks
options.rationale: Ablative therapy is reserved for primary hyperthyroidism with high uptake; in postpartum thyroiditis radioactive iodine is contraindicated and ineffective because uptake is already suppressed. (Source: ESA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guidance)

options.text: Initiate levothyroxine 50 micrograms daily and titrate to normalise TSH within 6 weeks
options.rationale: Levothyroxine is not indicated during the thyrotoxic phase and may exacerbate symptoms; treatment in the hypothyroid phase is reserved for symptomatic women or those planning pregnancy. (Source: ESA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guidance)

options.text: Commence propranolol 20 mg three times daily for symptoms and repeat thyroid function tests in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Postpartum thyroiditis is identified by transient thyrotoxicosis with low radioiodine uptake and positive anti-TPO; symptomatic beta-blockade and serial thyroid function tests are first-line, with an expected hypothyroid phase before recovery in most women. (Source: ESA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guidance)

options.text: Refer to a thyroid surgeon for total thyroidectomy to prevent recurrence in future pregnancies
options.rationale: Surgery is not indicated for postpartum thyroiditis; the natural history is self-limiting and recurrence in future pregnancies is best managed by surveillance, not pre-emptive resection. (Source: ESA Postpartum Thyroiditis Guidance)

explanation.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis affects 5 to 10 percent of women within 12 months of birth, typically in those with positive anti-TPO antibodies. The classical course is a thyrotoxic phase from 6 weeks to 6 months due to release of preformed hormone, followed by a hypothyroid phase from 3 to 12 months, with most women returning to euthyroid status. Low radioiodine uptake distinguishes it from Graves disease. Management of the thyrotoxic phase is symptomatic with propranolol; thionamides and radioactive iodine are not indicated. The hypothyroid phase warrants levothyroxine if symptomatic, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding poorly. Long-term annual TSH monitoring is recommended as 20 percent develop permanent hypothyroidism.
key_points: Postpartum thyroiditis is destructive thyroiditis with low radioiodine uptake
Anti-TPO positive women are at highest risk
Thyrotoxic phase is managed with beta blockade only
Annual TSH surveillance is required because permanent hypothyroidism develops in about 20 percent
explanation.pearls: Low radioiodine uptake plus positive anti-TPO in the first year after birth equals postpartum thyroiditis. Reach for propranolol, not carbimazole.

stem:
A 26-year-old woman who recently arrived from Papua New Guinea attends a refugee health clinic for routine screening. She is asymptomatic, has no cough, no fevers, and no weight loss. Her chest X-ray is reported as normal with no apical scarring or cavitation. A QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus assay returns positive. She is HIV negative on rapid testing and not pregnant.

Her vaccination history includes BCG in infancy. She is not currently taking any medications and her sputum culture cannot be collected as she has no productive cough.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Well; chest clear; no lymphadenopathy; no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: CXR: normal lung fields; QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus: positive; HIV: negative; LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: No treatment is needed because she has had BCG and a positive QuantiFERON likely reflects vaccination
options.rationale: Interferon-gamma release assays such as QuantiFERON are not affected by BCG vaccination; a positive result reflects Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection and warrants management. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

options.text: Commence empirical four-drug therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol
options.rationale: RIPE is treatment for active tuberculosis; the patient is asymptomatic with a normal chest X-ray, consistent with latent infection that does not require multi-drug therapy. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

options.text: Prescribe isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine 25 mg daily and monthly LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Australian guidance treats latent TB with isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months plus pyridoxine to prevent peripheral neuropathy, with monthly LFTs to detect hepatitis; this regimen reduces lifetime progression to active TB by approximately 70 percent. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

options.text: Repeat the QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus in 3 months and treat only if the result remains positive
options.rationale: Repeating an already positive interferon-gamma release assay does not add information and delays treatment that prevents progression to active tuberculosis. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

options.text: Refer for bronchoscopy and bronchoalveolar lavage to obtain a definitive microbiological diagnosis
options.rationale: Invasive sampling is reserved for patients with clinical, radiological, or microbiological features of active tuberculosis; this patient has none. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

explanation.summary: Latent tuberculosis infection is asymptomatic Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection without radiological or microbiological evidence of active disease. QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus and other interferon-gamma release assays are unaffected by BCG vaccination. Once active disease is excluded by a normal chest X-ray and clinical assessment, Australian guidance recommends isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine to prevent neuropathy, with monthly liver function tests to detect drug-induced hepatitis. Alternative shorter regimens include 4 months of rifampicin or 3 months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine. Treatment reduces the lifetime risk of progression to active tuberculosis by about 70 percent.
key_points: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is not affected by BCG vaccination
Latent TB is treated with isoniazid 9 months plus pyridoxine in AU first-line
Active disease must be excluded by CXR and symptoms before treating latent
Monthly LFT monitoring captures isoniazid hepatitis early
explanation.pearls: Add pyridoxine 25 mg with every isoniazid course to prevent peripheral neuropathy, especially in pregnant women, diabetics, and people with HIV.

sources.label: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee Australia: Latent TB

stem:
An 11-year-old girl with a 2-year history of migraine without aura attends a paediatric review with her mother. Headaches occur 4 to 5 times per month, last 6 hours each, and force her to lie down in a dark room. She has missed 6 days of school in the last term. Her abortive regimen is paracetamol with ibuprofen, taken at headache onset, which only partially helps.

She uses no triptans. There are no red flags on history or examination. Her mother has migraine. She is well in herself and her growth and development are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 110/68, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Normal general and neurological examination; no papilloedema; no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; headache diary kept by mother showing 4 to 5 attacks per month

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue paracetamol and ibuprofen for acute attacks and review with a headache diary in 6 months
options.rationale: Attack frequency above 4 per month with school absence meets the threshold for prophylaxis in paediatric migraine; ongoing acute therapy alone underservices her. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Refer for urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the brain before any prophylactic agent is started
options.rationale: MRI is indicated for red flags such as new neurological signs, morning vomiting, occipital location with neck pain, or rapidly escalating severity; routine prophylaxis decisions do not require imaging in the absence of red flags. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg nocte and titrate the dose at 4 week intervals as tolerated
options.rationale: Amitriptyline at 25 mg in an 11 year old is an excessive starting dose with cardiac and anticholinergic risk; AU paediatric guidance favours pizotifen or propranolol first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Prescribe oral sumatriptan as routine PRN abortive therapy without any prophylactic medication
options.rationale: Triptans are appropriate adjunct abortive therapy in adolescents but do not address the frequency that drives school absence; prophylaxis is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Commence pizotifen 0.5 mg nocte, optimise sleep, hydration, screen time, and review in 8 weeks
options.rationale: Paediatric migraine prophylaxis is indicated for 4 or more attacks per month or significant school absence; pizotifen 0.5 mg nocte plus lifestyle measures is an Australian first-line option, with review at 8 weeks for response and adverse effects. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

explanation.summary: Prophylaxis is indicated in paediatric migraine when there are 4 or more attacks per month, significant school absence, or abortive failure. First-line options in Australia are pizotifen or propranolol, both started at low dose and titrated. Lifestyle interventions including regular sleep, hydration, meal timing, and screen-time review are essential adjuncts. Amitriptyline and topiramate are second-line and require closer monitoring for adverse effects. Imaging is reserved for red flags. Treatment success is measured by attack frequency, severity, and functional impact, not by symptom abolition alone.
key_points: Prophylaxis threshold in paediatric migraine is 4+ attacks per month or significant school absence
First-line agents are pizotifen and propranolol
Lifestyle measures are core, not optional adjuncts
MRI is for red flags only
explanation.pearls: Pizotifen 0.5 mg nocte titrated to 1.5 mg is a reasonable starting prophylactic in paediatric migraine; counsel about weight gain and somnolence.

stem: A 64-year-old woman attends her general practice with a 4 week history of intermittent fresh red vaginal bleeding, 13 years after her last menstrual period. She has never had hormone replacement therapy. Her body mass index is 34 kg/m^2 and she has type 2 diabetes treated with metformin. She had two uncomplicated vaginal deliveries and her cervical screening test 2 years ago was HPV negative. There is no family history of breast or gynaecological malignancy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76, BP 138/82, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen; speculum: small amount of fresh blood at the cervical os, cervix benign in appearance; bimanual: bulky retroverted uterus, no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Recent cervical screening test HPV negative; BMI 34; HbA1c 7.4 percent

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Reassure her that this is likely vaginal atrophy and prescribe topical oestrogen with review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Any postmenopausal bleed is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise; reassurance without investigation misses up to 10 percent of women with malignancy. (Source: Cancer Council Australia: Endometrial Cancer)

options.text: Arrange transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness, with hysteroscopy and biopsy if thickness exceeds 4 mm
options.rationale: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line investigation in postmenopausal bleeding; an endometrial thickness of 4 mm or less in a woman not on hormone therapy is reassuring, while thickness above 4 mm or persistent bleeding requires hysteroscopy and endometrial biopsy. (Source: Cancer Council Australia: Endometrial Cancer)

options.text: Send a serum CA-125 and arrange contrast computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis as primary workup
options.rationale: CA-125 is non-specific in early endometrial cancer and CT does not visualise the endometrium reliably; transvaginal ultrasound is the appropriate first investigation. (Source: Cancer Council Australia: Endometrial Cancer)

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test for HPV and reflex liquid-based cytology as primary investigation
options.rationale: Cervical screening targets cervical cancer; postmenopausal bleeding requires endometrial assessment, which is not addressed by an HPV test. (Source: Cancer Council Australia: Endometrial Cancer)

options.text: Refer directly for magnetic resonance imaging of the pelvis before any other investigation is performed
options.rationale: MRI is reserved for staging once a tissue diagnosis of endometrial cancer is established; primary diagnostic workup uses transvaginal ultrasound and tissue sampling. (Source: Cancer Council Australia: Endometrial Cancer)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise and warrants urgent investigation. Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line test, with an endometrial thickness of 4 mm or less providing reassurance in women not on hormone therapy. Persistent bleeding or thickness above 4 mm requires hysteroscopy with endometrial biopsy. Obesity, type 2 diabetes, nulliparity, late menopause, and unopposed oestrogen exposure are major risk factors. CA-125, CT, and MRI have no role in primary workup; MRI is used after tissue diagnosis for staging. About 10 percent of women presenting with postmenopausal bleeding have endometrial cancer.
key_points: Any postmenopausal bleed warrants TVUS as first-line investigation
Endometrial thickness above 4 mm needs hysteroscopy and biopsy
Obesity and T2DM increase endometrial cancer risk
MRI is for staging only, after tissue diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Even one episode of postmenopausal bleeding mandates a TVUS within 2 weeks; an endometrial thickness above 4 mm always escalates to hysteroscopy and biopsy.

stem:
A 68-year-old retired carpenter is referred to neurology with a 12 month history of progressive right hand tremor at rest, micrographia, and slowing of fine finger movements. Examination shows asymmetric resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia of right hand finger tapping, and a slowed gait with reduced right arm swing. There is no postural instability and no autonomic or cognitive impairment.

An MRI brain has excluded a structural cause. He continues part-time consultancy and reports that his tremor is interfering with this work. His UPDRS motor score is 22.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72, BP 138/82 lying with no postural drop, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Asymmetric resting tremor right > left; cogwheel rigidity; bradykinesia; reduced right arm swing; no postural instability
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: normal; thyroid function normal; copper studies normal; UPDRS-III 22

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Commence carbidopa-levodopa at a low dose with gradual titration to control symptoms while maintaining function
options.rationale: In a 68 year old whose symptoms are interfering with work, levodopa provides the most reliable symptomatic benefit with the lowest short-term adverse effect burden; concerns about long-term motor complications do not justify delaying treatment that preserves function. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Initiate the dopamine agonist pramipexole and titrate as tolerated to minimise long-term motor fluctuations
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists are preferred in younger patients to defer motor complications; in a 68 year old they carry significant risk of impulse control disorders, somnolence, and oedema with limited additional benefit. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Start the MAO-B inhibitor rasagiline as monotherapy and defer dopamine therapy until functional impairment increases
options.rationale: MAO-B inhibitor monotherapy provides only modest symptomatic benefit; with established functional impairment and a UPDRS of 22, deferring more effective therapy is not appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence amantadine 100 mg twice daily as initial therapy with neurology review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Amantadine has weak symptomatic effect in early Parkinson disease and is typically reserved for treating later-stage dyskinesia rather than first-line motor symptoms. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Defer all medication and refer for structured exercise therapy as the sole intervention for 6 months
options.rationale: Exercise is an important adjunct in Parkinson disease but does not substitute for symptomatic therapy in a patient whose tremor is interfering with paid work. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Initial pharmacotherapy in idiopathic Parkinson disease balances symptomatic benefit against the long-term risk of motor complications. Levodopa is the most effective symptomatic agent, has the lowest short-term adverse effect burden, and is preferred in patients over 65 or those with significant functional impairment. Dopamine agonists are favoured in younger patients to defer motor fluctuations but carry impulse control disorder, somnolence, and oedema risks that limit their use in older patients. MAO-B inhibitors provide modest symptomatic benefit. Amantadine is used for dyskinesia later in disease. Levodopa-sparing strategies are no longer recommended in older patients with functional impairment.
key_points: Levodopa is first-line in older patients with functional impairment
Dopamine agonists carry impulse control disorder risk and are avoided in the elderly
MAO-B inhibitors give only modest symptomatic benefit as monotherapy
Withholding effective therapy compromises function without preventing motor complications
explanation.pearls: In a Parkinson patient over 65 whose symptoms threaten function, start levodopa at the lowest effective dose. Reserve dopamine agonists for younger patients with intact impulse control.

stem: A 5-year-old previously well girl is brought to the emergency department after her parents noticed widespread pinpoint red spots and bruising for 24 hours. Two weeks ago she had a self-limiting febrile viral illness. There has been no nose bleed, no mucosal bleeding, and no haematuria. She is otherwise well and meeting milestones. On examination she is afebrile and well-appearing with diffuse petechiae over her trunk and limbs, scattered bruises, and no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. Her platelet count is 8 x10^9/L with normal white cells, normal haemoglobin, and a normal blood film aside from large platelets.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7, BP 100/62
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing; widespread petechiae and bruises; no mucosal bleeding; no hepatosplenomegaly; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 125, WCC 7.5, platelets 8 x10^9/L; blood film shows large platelets, otherwise normal; CRP 4

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg as a single dose to raise the platelet count rapidly
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved for clinically significant bleeding or platelet counts below 20 with mucosal bleeding; this child has cutaneous signs only and a count of 8 alone does not mandate IVIG. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 4 mg/kg/day for 4 days with a planned haematology review at the end of the course
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are an alternative pharmacological treatment when intervention is needed but are not first-line in well-appearing children with cutaneous-only ITP. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Perform an urgent bone marrow aspirate and trephine to exclude acute leukaemia before any therapy
options.rationale: Routine bone marrow examination is not required in typical paediatric ITP with isolated thrombocytopenia and normal red and white cells; it is reserved for atypical features or non-response to first-line therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Observe with bleeding precautions, avoid contact sports, and review the next day with platelet count and clinical assessment
options.rationale: Paediatric ITP with cutaneous-only bleeding is managed conservatively because spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 80 percent within 6 months; treatment is reserved for clinically significant bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

options.text: Transfuse a single adult unit of pooled platelets to raise the count above 50 x10^9/L for safety
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is ineffective in ITP because transfused platelets are also destroyed, and it is reserved for life-threatening bleeding only. (Source: RCH CPG: Immune Thrombocytopenia)

explanation.summary: Acute immune thrombocytopenia in children typically follows a viral illness and presents with isolated thrombocytopenia and cutaneous bleeding signs in a well-looking child. Spontaneous remission occurs in approximately 80 percent within 6 months. Management is conservative when bleeding is cutaneous only, regardless of platelet count, with bleeding precautions and avoidance of contact sports. Indications for pharmacological therapy include mucosal or major bleeding, with IVIG, corticosteroids, or anti-D as first-line options. Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening bleeding. Bone marrow examination is required only with atypical features such as cytopenias in other lineages or non-response to first-line treatment.
key_points: Paediatric ITP with cutaneous-only bleeding is observed, not treated
Pharmacological treatment is reserved for mucosal or major bleeding
Platelet transfusion is ineffective except in life-threatening bleeding
Bone marrow is needed only for atypical features or treatment failure
explanation.pearls: Platelet count alone does not drive treatment in paediatric ITP; bleeding severity does. A well child with petechiae and a count of 5 is still managed conservatively.

stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 is admitted at 29+0 weeks gestation with progressive generalised oedema over 5 days, breathlessness on minimal exertion, and a 6 kg weight gain. A foetal medicine ultrasound that morning confirms severe foetal hydrops with ascites, pleural effusions, and scalp oedema. Her blood pressure is 152/96, urinalysis shows 3+ protein, platelets are 95, and uric acid is elevated. She has not received antenatal steroids in this pregnancy. The cause of foetal hydrops remains under investigation, but the foetus has known severe anaemia.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 102, BP 152/96, SpO2 95% on 2 L nasal prongs, RR 24, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Maternal facial puffiness and pitting peripheral oedema; bibasal crackles; fundus consistent with dates plus polyhydramnios
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 95, uric acid raised, ALT mildly raised, urine PCR 350 mg/mmol; foetal ultrasound: severe hydrops with ascites and pleural effusions

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Initiate intravenous magnesium sulfate and oral methyldopa, and continue conservative management until 34 weeks
options.rationale: Mirror syndrome resolves only with delivery or resolution of the foetal hydrops; conservative management to 34 weeks risks maternal pulmonary oedema and severe pre-eclampsia complications. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan urgent caesarean delivery within 6 hours and forgo antenatal corticosteroids because of maternal compromise
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection are still given where time permits because they substantially improve neonatal outcomes; complete omission is not standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Treat hypertension, administer antenatal corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate, then deliver within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Mirror syndrome is a pre-eclampsia like state driven by foetal hydrops and resolves only with delivery; while time allows, hypertension is controlled, corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate are given for foetal benefit, and delivery is planned within 24 to 48 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Perform intrauterine transfusion immediately and continue antenatal monitoring without planning delivery
options.rationale: Intrauterine transfusion is part of foetal therapy in selected hydrops aetiologies but does not reliably reverse maternal mirror syndrome quickly enough to defer delivery in this presentation. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence intravenous furosemide and fluid restriction as the primary intervention for the maternal oedema
options.rationale: Diuresis treats a symptom and does not address the underlying maternal disease; aggressive diuresis can worsen intravascular depletion in pre-eclampsia spectrum disorders. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Mirror syndrome describes maternal pre-eclampsia like illness occurring in association with foetal hydrops, named because maternal oedema mirrors foetal hydrops. It carries significant maternal risk including pulmonary oedema, severe hypertension, and progression to eclampsia, and resolves only with delivery or with reversal of the underlying foetal disease. While time permits, maternal hypertension is controlled, antenatal corticosteroids are given for lung maturity, and magnesium sulfate is given for neuroprotection in deliveries below 30 weeks gestation. Delivery is the only definitive maternal treatment and should generally be planned within 24 to 48 hours.
key_points: Mirror syndrome is pre-eclampsia like illness associated with foetal hydrops
Maternal oedema, hypertension, proteinuria and HELLP-like changes occur together
Delivery is the only definitive treatment for the maternal disease
Where time allows, give corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate before delivering
explanation.pearls: Maternal oedema with foetal hydrops is mirror syndrome until proven otherwise; delivery is the maternal cure, not diuresis or expectant management.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Victoria: Mirror Syndrome

stem: A 62-year-old man with a 20 pack-year smoking history presents with 3 weeks of progressive right-sided breathlessness and a dry cough. He has lost 4 kg unintentionally. Chest X-ray shows a moderate right pleural effusion with no other parenchymal abnormality. A diagnostic thoracentesis is performed under ultrasound guidance. The pleural fluid is straw coloured. Pleural protein is 38 g/L, pleural lactate dehydrogenase is 280 U/L, pleural glucose is 4.2 mmol/L, and pleural pH is 7.42. Serum protein is 68 g/L and serum LDH is 200 U/L.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 22, SpO2 94% room air, BP 128/76, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Stony dullness, reduced breath sounds, and reduced vocal resonance over the right base; no clubbing or lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Pleural protein 38 g/L, LDH 280 U/L; serum protein 68 g/L, LDH 200 U/L; pleural glucose 4.2, pH 7.42

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate interpretation and next step?

options.text: Transudate consistent with cardiac failure; commence furosemide and arrange outpatient echocardiogram
options.rationale: A pleural to serum protein ratio of 0.56 and LDH ratio of 1.40 meet Light criteria for an exudate; transudate is not consistent with these values and cardiac failure is unlikely to cause weight loss. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Transudate consistent with hypoalbuminaemia; commence albumin replacement and review with nutrition
options.rationale: Pleural protein is high at 38 g/L; transudates have protein below 25 g/L and a ratio below 0.5; hypoalbuminaemia is not the mechanism here. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Equivocal sample; repeat thoracentesis in 48 hours and review with results of a paired serum sample
options.rationale: The exudate criteria are unequivocally met by both protein and LDH ratios; repeat tap is not required to confirm exudate status. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Chylothorax most likely; check pleural triglycerides and consider lymphatic imaging before further workup
options.rationale: Chylothorax presents with milky fluid and elevated triglycerides; a straw-coloured fluid with these biochemistry values does not fit chylothorax. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Exudate by Light criteria; arrange contrast CT chest and discuss with respiratory for further investigation
options.rationale: Light criteria classify an effusion as exudate if pleural-to-serum protein ratio exceeds 0.5, pleural-to-serum LDH ratio exceeds 0.6, or pleural LDH exceeds two thirds of the upper limit of normal serum LDH; the ratios here are 0.56 and 1.40, mandating workup for an exudative cause including malignancy. (Source: BTS Pleural Disease Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Light criteria classify a pleural effusion as exudative if any one of the following is met: pleural-to-serum protein ratio above 0.5, pleural-to-serum LDH ratio above 0.6, or pleural LDH exceeding two thirds of the upper limit of normal serum LDH. Exudates require investigation for cause, with malignancy, parapneumonic effusion, tuberculosis, and connective tissue disease leading the differential. In a smoker with unintentional weight loss and a unilateral exudate, malignancy is the leading concern and contrast CT chest with pleural review for image-guided pleural biopsy is appropriate. Cytology of a single tap is positive in only 60 percent of malignant effusions, so repeat sampling and biopsy may be required.
key_points: Light criteria identify exudate when any one ratio threshold is breached
Smoker with weight loss and unilateral exudate is malignancy until proven otherwise
Cytology sensitivity is around 60 percent on a single tap
Pleural biopsy is often needed to secure the diagnosis
explanation.pearls: An exudate by Light criteria in a smoker with weight loss escalates to contrast CT and respiratory review; do not rely on a single negative cytology.
